seller_posture.evidence: Arizona has enough public cap flexibility that Dante Stills should be treated as a roster-role monitor, not a financially forced seller lane.

position_group_stance.stance: Dante Stills is a clean one-year depth-contract monitor because the buyer gets a cheap interior body, but Arizona needs a football reason to move him.
position_group_stance.seller_depth_notes: Arizona would lose a low-cost interior rotation option and has no cap-pressure reason to create the hole.
position_group_stance.sell_threshold: Upgrade only if camp usage or another defensive-line addition makes Stills clearly outside the game-day rotation.

desired_return_types: Day-three pick premium over replacement-level depth.
Player-for-player swap only if it solves a sharper roster need.
avoided_deal_types: Selling young defensive-line depth for ordinary cap relief.
NFC contender help without a clear draft premium.
market_preferences.division_rivalry_friction: No direct division friction with the Giants; conference trade still needs a draft premium because Arizona is not cap-forced.

trade_triggers.trigger: Cardinals add or elevate another interior lineman ahead of Stills.
trade_triggers.implication: Stills becomes a plausible check-call target because the roster cost falls below the draft-pick return.

trade_triggers.trigger: Arizona shifts from buyer-hold posture to asset collection after camp or early-season losses.
trade_triggers.implication: A one-year depth contract can move from monitor to check-call.

availability_validation.check: Confirm Stills' interior-snap role, game-day status, and whether Arizona views him as expendable after defensive-line cuts.
Verify the one-year trade economics and no hidden guarantee blocker before pricing a day-three pick.

no_trade_guardrails.guardrail: Do not lead with Stills unless Arizona has a roster-role reason to sell; cap fit alone is not enough.

seller_posture.evidence: The Rams have multiple expensive veteran one-year levers, but a normal buyer posture means only non-core or surplus veterans should be treated as check-call lanes.

position_group_stance.stance: Davante Adams is a high-impact but price-sensitive one-year call because the buyer gets 2026 production without 2027 salary, while Los Angeles loses a top passing-game piece.
position_group_stance.seller_depth_notes: The sell case depends on whether the Rams prefer draft value and cap room over carrying an expensive veteran receiver for a run.
position_group_stance.sell_threshold: Treat as monitor or check-call only if Los Angeles signals that a premium pick return matters more than the short-term receiver role.

desired_return_types: Premium or conditional draft capital for a one-year veteran starter
Deals that clear current-year salary without adding future guarantees
avoided_deal_types: Selling a top offensive weapon cheaply during a competitive window
Veteran-for-veteran swaps that add 2027 commitments
market_preferences.division_rivalry_friction: No division issue with NYG; Los Angeles should still price a featured receiver as a meaningful football loss.

trade_triggers.trigger: Rams receiver depth holds up and the front office prioritizes draft capital over carrying a costly one-year veteran.
trade_triggers.implication: Adams-style calls move from monitor to check-call.

availability_validation.check: Confirm whether Adams is a core starter in the weekly plan or a negotiable veteran contract.
Confirm no extension or restructure is required at acquisition.

no_trade_guardrails.guardrail: Do not frame Adams as available only because the contract is one-year; the seller case needs a real pick-value or depth trigger.

recent_significant_trades.summary: Public transaction history should be verified before external use; internal graph captures New York Giants as a team with recurring roster-lever decisions.

seller_posture.evidence: Giants should be treated as the buyer in this scenario; current cap rows make Jon Runyan the cleanest salary-out lever before premium DB or core edge pieces.

position_group_stance.stance: Jon Runyan is the first salary-out conversation because his one-year ledger gives clean 2026 trade room, but moving him thins the interior offensive line.
position_group_stance.seller_depth_notes: Current roster/cap rows leave roughly 15 offensive-line bodies after a Runyan move, with Thomas, Eluemunor, and Mauigoa as the named higher-cap anchors.
position_group_stance.sell_threshold: Only move Runyan if the acquired interior defender is a true pressure upgrade or if a separate low-cost OL replacement is identified.

position_group_stance.stance: Paulson Adebo, Jevon Holland, and Greg Newsome II create cap room, but trading a starting-grade DB is higher football cost than moving Runyan.
position_group_stance.seller_depth_notes: DB is one of the richer Giants position groups, but the top names are real defensive starters rather than painless cap ballast.
position_group_stance.sell_threshold: Use a DB only if the DT return is materially better than the Runyan-plus-pick path and the staff accepts the secondary depth hit.

desired_return_types: Expiring veteran interior defender
Pick-led rental structure
Salary-out only from lower-pain contracts
avoided_deal_types: Extension required at acquisition
Restructure that pushes money into 2027
Cap-relief trade involving Burns, Thomas, Carter, or Nabers
market_preferences.division_rivalry_friction: NFC East deals should carry extra price and politics; do not treat PHI or WAS targets as easy lanes without stronger seller evidence.

trade_triggers.trigger: A seller values a middle-round pick over holding an expiring interior defender.
trade_triggers.implication: Move from monitor to check-call if the DT does not require new money beyond 2026.

availability_validation.check: Confirm the seller would move the target without requiring an extension, restructure, or 2027 salary commitment.
Confirm Runyan replacement plan before using him as the salary-out piece.

no_trade_guardrails.guardrail: Do not use Brian Burns, Andrew Thomas, Abdul Carter, or Malik Nabers as cap relief; current rows show bad economics or core-player cost.
